title: Baseball

jokes.text: 'Food-nomenal. Really. It really is.' — staffer sincerely complimenting Chick-fil-A to the VP
jokes.setup: A White House staffer is supposed to be briefing the VP on a serious food initiative narrative
jokes.punchline: His contribution is the portmanteau 'food-nomenal,' delivered with complete sincerity and enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: A solid throat-clearing joke establishing the absurdity of the VP's situation. The made-up portmanteau signals that the people around her are not exactly the best and brightest. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'My bladder was like a fuckin' blimp.'
jokes.setup: Staffer complaining about being stuck in construction traffic on the way to a dignified White House event
jokes.punchline: The image of his bladder as a blimp — an oddly specific, vivid, and slightly too-personal overshare in a professional context
jokes.explanation: The blimp simile is vivid and funny in its vulgarity and oversharing. The contrast of a blimp with the professional setting lands. Competent Iannucci grotesque-body-image humor.

jokes.text: 'If you ever want me to confess, don't rip out my fingernails, just make me eat like a vegan for a fuckin' week.'
jokes.setup: Selina is suffering through a healthy-food initiative event she finds utterly soul-destroying
jokes.punchline: She frames vegan eating as a torture method more effective than physical violence — a hyperbolic but character-perfect expression of her contempt
jokes.explanation: This is exactly the kind of line that defines Selina — she expresses genuine political/professional despair through absurd hyperbole that could only come from a superficial, status-obsessed politician. The torture framing is dark and unexpected, economy is tight, and it tells us everything about her character. Strong line.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, feel my soul slide out of my ass.'
jokes.setup: An aide suggests Selina should engage more with the attendees to get through the event
jokes.punchline: Selina completes the thought with a grotesque, visceral image of her existential despair
jokes.explanation: The soul-sliding-out-of-ass image is perfectly Veep: bodily, specific, despairing. It's economical (six words, maximum disgust) and thoroughly character-integrated. Selina's spiritual deterioration expressed through scatology — that's the show's DNA.

jokes.text: 'This smells like the inside of a taxi cab.' — Selina about the healthy food
jokes.setup: Selina is handed some healthy food item at the initiative event
jokes.punchline: Her immediate sensory rejection: it smells like a taxi cab
jokes.explanation: A quick, competent sensory insult. The taxi cab comparison is specific enough to be funny. Functional character beat.

jokes.text: 'Orgasmic's more like it, right?'
jokes.setup: A vendor proudly describes food as 'all local and all organic' in a professional food-initiative setting with strangers
jokes.punchline: Selina mishears or riffs 'organic' as 'orgasmic,' turning a wholesome pitch into an accidental sexual innuendo, delivered with breezy confidence
jokes.explanation: The organic/orgasmic slip is a well-worn comedic device but Selina's breezy delivery and the professional setting make it work. The follow-up 'I'll have some of what you're having' from someone in the group (a Katz's Deli riff) gives it a second beat. Solid cringe with good economy.

jokes.text: 'I'll have some of what you're having.' — bystander response to Selina's 'orgasmic' comment
jokes.setup: Selina has just said the food is 'orgasmic'
jokes.punchline: A bystander immediately riffs with the When Harry Met Sally 'I'll have what she's having' line, landing a second beat on the innuendo
jokes.explanation: A borrowed pop-culture punchline — the When Harry Met Sally reference — that extends joke #6. Not original, but correctly deployed. The joke belongs to the setup (Selina), not this line.

jokes.text: Gary's dad would 'rather me work here' — 'In catering?' / 'No, a baseball stadium.'
jokes.setup: Gary mentions his dad has little respect for his job as the VP's personal aide and would prefer him in a more masculine career
jokes.punchline: Amy mishears the implication as 'catering' — an equally demeaning alternative — before Gary clarifies it's a baseball stadium
jokes.explanation: The catering misdirection is sharp — Amy's instinct to place Gary in equally emasculating work is a quick character beat for both of them. Clean setup/punchline with a good false landing.

jokes.text: 'I meant for him.' — Amy's response after Gary says it must be tough that his dad doesn't respect him
jokes.setup: Gary laments that it's tough that his father disrespects his job
jokes.punchline: Amy clarifies she meant it must be tough for the dad — implying Gary's situation IS as pathetic as his father thinks
jokes.explanation: Three-word punchline that is devastating. The structure is perfect: Gary invites sympathy, Amy flips the entire premise by siding with the dismissive father. It requires a beat of processing before the cruelty lands. Perfectly economical, entirely character-consistent for Amy, and quotable. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Selina complaining about Ken the photographer's small, touchy hands and comparing him to Penthouse
jokes.setup: An overly tactile photographer named Ken keeps touching Selina during the shoot
jokes.punchline: Selina hisses to Amy about his 'feely, little, touchy, hot hands' and says someone needs to tell him he's working for the White House, not Penthouse
jokes.explanation: The White House/Penthouse rhyme is the engine here — a neat wordplay that justifies the complaint. 'Feely, little, touchy, hot hands' is pleasantly specific and neurotic. Solid mid-tier Veep cringe.

jokes.text: 'Someone needs to tell him that he's working with the White House and not Penthouse.' — Selina about Ken the photographer's constant touching
jokes.setup: Ken keeps touching Selina during shoots; she complains about his 'feely, little, touchy, hot hands'
jokes.punchline: The White House / Penthouse rhyme lands as a sharp institutional-register joke
jokes.explanation: The rhyme is sharp and the institutional joke lands — the Vice President reduced to a Penthouse model by an overeager photographer. The preceding escalation ('feely, little, touchy, hot hands') sets it up well. Economy is strong; punchline arrives cleanly.

jokes.text: 'You stole a car?' — Amy's guess at what Selina's big confession is
jokes.setup: Selina is nervously building up to a major confession involving a lot of wine and Springsteen getting 'out of hand'
jokes.punchline: Amy's first guess is car theft — a completely wrong but oddly plausible guess about Selina's behavior
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that also characterizes Selina: Amy knows her boss well enough that car theft feels plausible as an outcome of wine and Springsteen. The specificity of that guess (not 'did something bad' but 'stole a car') is what makes it funny. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Selina's defense of her fertility: 'I could probably have unprotected sex with 30 men, one right after the other, and it would be totally fine.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to downplay the likelihood that she's actually pregnant by asserting she's still fertile but the odds are low
jokes.punchline: Her argument escalates to an absurdly specific hypothetical involving 30 consecutive sexual partners — framed as a reassuring fertility fact
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional Veep writing. The logic is impeccable in its wrongness: Selina is trying to argue low probability but instead describes a scenario that would be mortifying for any VP. The number '30' is specific enough to feel deliberate. Her immediate self-correction ('I mean, no, it wouldn't be totally fine') adds another beat. Peak character comedy — only Selina would say this, only in this show.

jokes.text: 'I mean, no, it wouldn't be totally fine. I mean, that would be a horrible thing.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just asserted that having unprotected sex with 30 men 'would be totally fine'
jokes.punchline: She immediately realizes the absurdity and walks it back — but only on moral grounds, not fertility grounds, which is its own comedy
jokes.explanation: The callback beat that extends joke #12. The correction is almost worse than the original statement — she's more worried about the optics of the 30-men scenario than the actual point about pregnancy. Character-perfect coda.

jokes.text: Amy: 'an unwed mother one aneurism away from the presidency? How do you think that plays?'
jokes.setup: Selina is musing about the possibility she might be pregnant
jokes.punchline: Amy's concern is not personal but purely political — she frames it as a narrative problem, using 'one aneurism away from the presidency' as a cold-blooded descriptor
jokes.explanation: 'One aneurism away from the presidency' is a great phrase — matter-of-fact about the president potentially dying, purely in service of a political optics argument. Amy's complete lack of empathy is the joke, and it's well-constructed.

jokes.text: Gary's bag has 'one big storage area and then 60 separate pockets' — the pocket quiz
jokes.setup: Gary is proudly demonstrating his ability to manage the VP's bag
jokes.punchline: The bag has 60 pockets, Gary can locate items without looking, and Amy tests him like a game show — revealing Gary's skills are entirely in service of trivial personal assistance
jokes.explanation: The pocket quiz is pure Gary character comedy — a man who has devoted his considerable memory and organizational skills entirely to fetching eye drops and wipes for a politician. The 'scented or unscented?' detail and the 'Bam!' are lovely. Running gag of Gary's pathetic competence.

jokes.text: 'Unless we go on some sort of special vacation to Mexico and... kill the doctor in the desert.'
jokes.setup: Amy tells Selina she'll have to keep the baby if she's pregnant; Selina starts to suggest an alternative
jokes.punchline: Selina's euphemism for abortion trails off into the surreal notion of killing a doctor in a Mexican desert — an absurdist escalation of the standard 'special vacation to Mexico' euphemism
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's standout dark comedy lines. It starts as a recognizable political euphemism ('special vacation to Mexico') and escalates to murder. The specificity — 'kill the doctor in the desert' — is absurdly cinematic. The pacing, with the scene cut mid-sentence for maximum comic effect, makes it a textbook Veep construction. High originality for its genre.

jokes.text: 'Father know? / What, are you kidding me? What am I, 15?'
jokes.setup: Amy asks if the father knows about the potential pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Selina is offended, taking 'father' to mean her own father rather than the baby's father — revealing a teenage-level panic underneath her VP authority
jokes.explanation: The misunderstanding is a classic device but it lands because it's revelatory: Selina's brain went immediately to her own father, suggesting she feels 15 anyway. The 'What am I, 15?' is a good self-aware beat.

jokes.text: Ken's aggressive 1D camera bragging: 'I have a 1D. It's more expensive, but it's a really nice camera.'
jokes.setup: Ken the photographer defends his equipment choice against Selina's implicit criticism
jokes.punchline: His defense escalates into 'you shoot hundreds of pictures of herons catching fish?' — an absurdly specific use case that reveals his camera knowledge is insecure and performative
jokes.explanation: The heron specificity is the best part — a glimpse into the world of competitive amateur photography that feels real and absurd. Ken as a minor irritant character is well-deployed here.

jokes.text: 'Business card bukkake'
jokes.setup: Dan is describing how the stadium networking event was useful for him
jokes.punchline: He uses the pornographic term 'bukkake' as a metaphor for being showered with business cards — a deliberately shocking and crude image delivered casually
jokes.explanation: Two words. Maximum impact. 'Business card bukkake' is the kind of phrase that makes a writers' room whoop — it's perfectly economical, shockingly original, and completely character-appropriate for Dan's crass ambition. It recontextualizes a professional activity through the most inappropriate possible metaphor. This is the episode's most quotable line and one of the best single jokes Veep has produced. Craft score matches the 8.0 anchor and exceeds it on economy and originality.

jokes.text: 'Razor blades, a large amount of pain killers.' — Dan suggests Selina's stadium purchases
jokes.setup: Dan suggests Selina should pick something up while at the stadium
jokes.punchline: He recommends suicide implements — a casual, deadpan suggestion about ending it all that doubles as commentary on how bad the VP's situation is
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep dark humor — suicide as a career option offered casually. Functional and character-appropriate for Dan. Not as original as 'business card bukkake' right before it.

jokes.text: 'One day you're gonna wake up and you're gonna find your lungs have turned to pastry.'
jokes.setup: Mike is eating on a video call, caught not working
jokes.punchline: Jonah's health warning takes an absurdist turn: not 'your lungs will fail' but 'your lungs will turn to pastry'
jokes.explanation: 'Lungs turned to pastry' is a wonderfully specific and grotesque image. It works as an absurdist health threat that somehow makes the danger more vivid, not less. The pastry connects to Mike's eating on camera. Good internal logic makes the absurdism land harder.

jokes.text: 'If it is, the best thing for her legacy is if she's assassinated before she starts showing.'
jokes.setup: Amy has just told Mike that Selina might be pregnant and asks what they should do
jokes.punchline: Mike's political calculus immediately lands on 'assassination before she shows' as the optimal legacy outcome
jokes.explanation: The assassination framing is perfectly dark — Mike's PR brain has run the numbers and concluded that dying before the bump shows is good for the brand. It's a sharp satire of political communications logic taken to its conclusion.

jokes.text: 'Okay. I'll guess I'll just add this to the press release. People can start taking bets.'
jokes.setup: Amy desperately warns Mike that the pregnancy situation must be kept secret
jokes.punchline: Mike sarcastically suggests putting it in the press release and letting people bet
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic retort. Functional but doesn't distinguish itself. Mike's sarcasm here is a bit routine.

jokes.text: 'It's gonna knock the socks off of some seven-year-olds.' — Amy's sign-off after the pregnancy conversation
jokes.setup: Amy has just disclosed a potential VP pregnancy to Mike; she signs off the conversation
jokes.punchline: She transitions immediately to describing the school visit with the most banal enthusiasm possible, as if the previous conversation never happened
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the jarring pivot from existential crisis to cheerful event promotion. 'Knock the socks off of some seven-year-olds' in that context is genuinely funny deflation. Solid Veep texture.

jokes.text: Gary on the phone with his dad: 'Listen, the veep and I are gonna have our photo taken with the Orioles players... Shit, yeah. Shit, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Gary, whose father thinks his job is pathetic, is trying to impress his dad by name-dropping the VP visit
jokes.punchline: His dad's reaction is so enthusiastic that Gary switches to jock-speak ('Shit, yeah. Shit, yeah.') — revealing the pathetic extent to which Gary is desperate for his father's approval
jokes.explanation: A callback that pays off the earlier father-disrespect setup. Gary instantly code-switching to 'shit, yeah' for dad approval is painfully observed and character-specific. The callback structure earns this more than the joke would alone.

jokes.text: 'Are you pregnant? Amy, come on, career-wise, that's like joining Scientology or getting a fucking neck tattoo.'
jokes.setup: Dan finds out Selina might be pregnant
jokes.punchline: His frame of reference is entirely career optics — pregnancy is categorized alongside Scientology and neck tattoos as reputation-destroyers
jokes.explanation: The Scientology/neck tattoo list is great — both are reputation-destroying choices that signal a kind of irreversible self-sabotage. The list form is well-deployed. Dan's complete absence of sympathy and reduction of a pregnancy to a PR problem is perfect character work.

jokes.text: 'Lion King DVD and two shots of ritalin.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy need to keep children entertained while Selina is delayed by the gas leak
jokes.punchline: Dan's child-management solution is to drug them — then explains 'That's how I was raised'
jokes.explanation: The Ritalin suggestion alone is dark but the 'That's how I was raised' kicker is the real punchline — it explains Dan's entire personality in six words while getting a second laugh. Economical double-beat structure.

jokes.text: 'I'd be the obvious choice for your replacement.' — Dan's first thought about Selina's pregnancy
jokes.setup: Dan has just heard Selina might be pregnant and references maternity leave
jokes.punchline: Without pause, he identifies himself as her replacement — purely opportunistic, completely self-serving, delivered as a reasonable statement
jokes.explanation: Pure Dan. The instant pivot from sympathy to self-promotion, framed as a logical observation, is the essence of his character. The deadpan delivery of 'obvious choice' is what makes it.

jokes.text: 'No. My first thought was, "there goes your figure," but I didn't say that because I thought it would be upsetting.'
jokes.setup: Amy accuses Dan of making his own promotion his first thought upon hearing about the pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Dan reveals his ACTUAL first thought was about Selina's figure — which he's now sharing, despite claiming he withheld it to be considerate
jokes.explanation: Structurally perfect: Dan defends himself by revealing something worse than what he's being accused of, while simultaneously patting himself on the back for his restraint. The self-awareness that makes him say the upsetting thing while claiming he didn't is layered and brilliantly written. This is genuinely excellent character comedy — Dan at his most Dan.

jokes.text: 'Thanks. You know, if it's a boy, maybe I'll name him after you. Call him fuck weasel.'
jokes.setup: Amy sarcastically thanks Dan for his 'support' about the pregnancy situation
jokes.punchline: She offers to name the baby after him — but the name she'd give him is 'fuck weasel'
jokes.explanation: 'Fuck weasel' is a highly specific and creative compound insult — the weasel component is doing real characterization work (Dan IS a weasel). The baby-naming frame is the elegant setup. Quotable and well-constructed.

jokes.text: 'Amy's practically having a baby about it.' — Selina's accidental pregnancy reference in front of officials
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to appear enthusiastic about the school visit while the pregnancy secret is being kept
jokes.punchline: She accidentally uses a pregnancy idiom ('having a baby about it') about Amy — who has been tasked with pregnancy tests — within earshot of strangers
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed accidental revelation: Selina uses a pregnancy idiom about Amy — the person most involved in concealing her pregnancy — in a public setting. 'Amy's practically having a baby about it' is the kind of Freudian deployment that Veep excels at. The panic it implies and the breezy way she moves past it make it funnier.

jokes.text: Dan networking with an eight-year-old: 'My dad works in the state department. / Really? What position? / Trade policy executive. / So he knows Roger Aldrige? / Are you networking with an eight-year-old? / Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Dan is supposed to be entertaining children while Selina is delayed
jokes.punchline: He immediately begins networking with a child whose father is in the State Department — treating the eight-year-old as a legitimate professional contact, and then openly admitting it
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. Dan's compulsive networking instinct is so strong it activates in the presence of an eight-year-old with a State Department parent. The deadpan 'Yeah.' when Amy asks if he's networking with a child — completely unembarrassed — is the perfect button. Could only be Dan. Among the episode's best character moments.

jokes.text: Mike at the press briefing: 'I'd like to free that boy from the prison of obesity'
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to explain the VP's health initiative at a press briefing, quoting Selina
jokes.punchline: The quote he uses — 'free that boy from the prison of obesity' — is a wildly insensitive way to describe a fat child seen on the campaign trail
jokes.explanation: 'Prison of obesity' is a perfect political buzzword construction that sounds vaguely empowering but is actually just mean. The anecdote framing — as a moving origin story for the policy — makes it more pointed.

jokes.text: The secret service agent fired for smiling — cologne excuse: 'It was Paco Rabanne. I mean, this is not the '80s anymore, right?'
jokes.setup: A reporter asks if a secret service agent was fired just for smiling at the VP
jokes.punchline: Mike deflects with the claim he was reassigned for his cologne (Paco Rabanne), then riffs on that as if it's a perfectly reasonable cause
jokes.explanation: Mike's spin tactics are always funny and the cologne excuse is a good specific absurdity. His improvised riff on Paco Rabanne being dated extends the joke well, though it slightly overstays its welcome.

jokes.text: 'Nobody wears that stuff. Sean might. You'd probably rather drink it, though. Right, Sean?'
jokes.setup: Mike is riffing about Paco Rabanne being dated at a press briefing, suddenly targeting a specific reporter (Sean) as a drunk
jokes.punchline: The pivot to accusing Sean of drinking cologne — with 'I've seen you' from Sean confirming he IS a drunk
jokes.explanation: The Sean-as-confirmed-drunk bit is a quick pleasure — the reporter's candid 'I'm a drunk' admission is an unexpected grace note. The cologne-to-drinking chain is a nimble comedic non-sequitur.

jokes.text: Okay, every which way but douche, I want you to answer my question now.
jokes.setup: Mike has been deflecting Leon's question with jokes and digressions
jokes.punchline: 'Every which way but douche' is a play on the Clint Eastwood film 'Every Which Way But Loose,' used as a colorful command to stop dicking around
jokes.explanation: A fun wordplay subversion of the Eastwood title that also functions as a precise insult. 'Douche' substituting for 'Loose' works well. Quotable and inventive enough to stand out.

jokes.text: 'Okay, every which way but douche, I want you to answer my question now.'
jokes.setup: Leon West (reporter) is frustrated with Mike's evasive press conference answers
jokes.punchline: He uses 'every which way but douche' — a riff on the Clint Eastwood film 'Every Which Way But Loose' substituting 'loose' with 'douche'
jokes.explanation: A sharp pop-culture wordplay that doubles as an insult. The Eastwood reference feels era-appropriate for a veteran reporter, and the substitution works both phonetically and conceptually. High economy.

jokes.text: 'I believe, ahem, you asked a boring question, Leon, and I gave a boring answer. That's like carbon offsetting.'
jokes.setup: Leon pushes back on Mike's evasive answer about the fired agent
jokes.punchline: Mike equates his boring answer to a boring question with carbon offsetting — an environmental concept applied to conversational ethics
jokes.explanation: 'That's like carbon offsetting' is a genuinely clever conceptual joke — the idea that a boring question earns a boring answer through some neutral exchange, like buying credits. The application of environmental economics to press evasion is original and Mike-appropriate. Strong line.

jokes.text: 'You're calling the vice president repugnant? That's a very emotive term, sir, and a little prissy, I might add.'
jokes.setup: Leon calls the VP's behavior 'repugnant' and Mike challenges the word choice
jokes.punchline: Mike calls 'repugnant' both emotive AND prissy simultaneously — a contradiction that reveals his spin instinct over-firing
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat — calling a word both too emotional and too precious is self-defeating spin, which is pure Mike. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'I spent a night in jail when I was 19 once. That was more fun than this.'
jokes.setup: Leon is stuck in the prolonged press briefing that has gone nowhere
jokes.punchline: He compares it unfavorably to a night in jail — jail was more fun
jokes.explanation: A tight one-liner. The 'once' at the end is a nice rhythmic touch suggesting it may have happened more than once. Solid but not remarkable.

jokes.text: 'Well, if we get trapped here another minute, I'm gonna kill you and eat you, because I will not eat another fuckin' falafel.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trapped in the stadium by the gas leak with the food-initiative people
jokes.punchline: Her threat to cannibalize whoever is nearest rather than eat more falafel — a perfect expression of her contempt for the health food event
jokes.explanation: The logic here is airtight: the threat of cannibalism is preferable to more falafel. It's character-specific (Selina's food snobbery), dark, and escalatory. 'I will not eat another fuckin' falafel' as the justification for murder is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Jimmy fuckin' Olsen, get lost before I shove that thing up your ass and give you another kind of red eye to worry about.'
jokes.setup: Ken the photographer is hovering too close to Selina again with his camera
jokes.punchline: Selina threatens to insert the camera rectally and produce a 'red eye' — a photography term reapplied anatomically, plus the Superman reference for the photographer
jokes.explanation: This payoff to Ken's running irritant arc is well-constructed. The 'red eye' double meaning (camera flash artifact / rectal viewing) is clever wordplay, and 'Jimmy fucking Olsen' is a perfectly specific Superman-universe reference for an annoying photographer. Memorable and quotable.

jokes.text: Hey, Jimmy fuckin' Olsen, get lost before I shove that thing up your ass and give you another kind of red eye to worry about.
jokes.setup: Ken the photographer is still hovering around Selina with his camera
jokes.punchline: The Superman reference ('Jimmy Olsen') plus the camera/red-eye/anal threat is a densely packed insult with multiple layers
jokes.explanation: Inventive compound insult. 'Jimmy Olsen' as a dismissive nickname works, and the wordplay on 'red eye' (photo defect / bodily threat) is clever. Shows Selina at her most creatively aggressive.

jokes.text: 'Could you lip-read earlier when I was talking about you to Amy? / No. / Oh, that's too bad. It was all good.'
jokes.setup: Gary reveals he can lip-read; Selina immediately worries he overheard her talking about him
jokes.punchline: When Gary says he couldn't, Selina immediately says it was 'all good' — an obvious lie, delivered with zero conviction
jokes.explanation: The 'it was all good' is a transparent lie that Gary almost certainly sees through but accepts because that's his relationship with Selina. The exchange works in three beats — revelation, relief, transparent lie — at excellent speed.

jokes.text: Could you lip-read earlier when I was talking about you to Amy? / No. / Oh, that's too bad. It was all good.
jokes.setup: Selina discovers Gary can lip-read; she's suddenly concerned about what he might have seen
jokes.punchline: When Gary says he couldn't see, Selina immediately lies — 'it was all good' — revealing she was almost certainly saying something awful about him
jokes.explanation: A great moment of character revelation. The structure is tight — panic, relief, immediate lie. 'It was all good' is a perfect Selina cover, simultaneously transparent to the audience and casually delivered. The 'That's too bad' before the reassurance is the comedic timing gift — she's disappointed she can't come clean even ironically.

jokes.text: Mike's pen cap — 'Can I get the pen cap, too? This came with a pen cap. / The pen cap. Where was the pen... in your mouth? Did you put it in your mouth?'
jokes.setup: Mike borrows a pen from Amy during a tense call about the pregnancy story
jokes.punchline: Mike obsessively pursues the pen cap — interrupting critical information flow — fixated on whether Amy put it in her mouth
jokes.explanation: The pen cap as an absurdly petty distraction during a crisis is classic Veep — minor logistical concerns overwhelming the room. The mouth-contact germaphobia makes Mike's neurosis visible.

jokes.text: Selina trying not to move her lips while Amy relays pregnancy news: 'Ugh, I can't move my lips.'
jokes.setup: Selina is surrounded by people and trying to communicate secretly with Amy about the pregnancy, aware that Gary (revealed to be a lip-reader) might be watching
jokes.punchline: She freeze-faces, can't move her lips, and has to speak through gritted teeth — a physical comedy bit in a high-stakes political moment
jokes.explanation: A callback to Gary's lip-reading reveal that generates physical comedy. The image of the VP speaking through locked teeth in a public setting is inherently funny. Visual comedy that benefits from the setup.

jokes.text: Gary's baby fantasy: 'I can carry the baby around in a little sling or something like that.'
jokes.setup: Gary is told the VP might be pregnant — a need-to-know secret
jokes.punchline: His first thought is not concern or strategy but an immediate fantasy about wearing the baby in a sling — revealing his servant/caretaker role would naturally extend to the baby
jokes.explanation: This is beautifully character-specific to Gary — his instinctive response to the pregnancy news is to imagine himself as an extension of the VP's parental unit, wearing her baby. The sling detail is perfect. It says everything about Gary's relationship to Selina in one image.

jokes.text: 'Is Jonah the dad? Is that why you look like you wanna drink a bottle of Drano?'
jokes.setup: Amy is standing near Dan looking miserable as the pregnancy crisis unfolds
jokes.punchline: Dan guesses that Jonah is the father — an absurd escalation — and uses 'drink a bottle of Drano' as a description of Amy's expression
jokes.explanation: 'Drink a bottle of Drano' is a vivid, specific expression of suicidal despair that works because Dan delivers it so casually. The Jonah-as-dad suggestion is absurd enough to get its own laugh.

jokes.text: 'I don't blame you. That's gonna be a long labor. They're gonna be pulling that kid out of you in shifts.'
jokes.setup: Dan has jokingly suggested Jonah might be the father, referencing Jonah's extreme height
jokes.punchline: The baby would be so large (due to Jonah's size) that it would require shift work to deliver
jokes.explanation: 'Pulling that kid out in shifts' is an inspired image — the shift-work metaphor applied to childbirth is unexpected and specific. It extends the Jonah-as-father premise to a logical absurd conclusion. Strong escalation structure.

jokes.text: Selina's baseball ignorance — 'all three players are starting pitchers': 'You don't have multiple starting pitchers. There's one pitcher's mound. You see one mound or three mounds?'
jokes.setup: Selina asks Jonah for background on the Orioles players so she can make small talk; Jonah lists three 'starting pitchers'
jokes.punchline: Selina, who knows nothing about baseball, correctly identifies that you can't have multiple starting pitchers and uses brutal logic to expose Jonah's cluelessness — 'Fuckin' idiot'
jokes.explanation: The irony that Selina, who knows nothing about baseball, still out-logics Jonah is a neat character reversal. The mound argument is solid. 'Fuckin' idiot' as the closing beat is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Gary on the phone with his dad: 'I'm with the team... my playas. Boom shaka laka. That's how they talk.'
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to impress his skeptical father by proving he's hanging with real athletes
jokes.punchline: His attempt at athlete-speak ('boom shaka laka') is immediately followed by his own gloss: 'That's how they talk' — which is painfully unhip and reveals the gap between Gary's world and the jock world his dad values
jokes.explanation: 'Boom shaka laka. That's how they talk.' is perfectly cringeworthy. The self-explanatory gloss demolishes any remaining coolness. This is Gary at his most pathetically endearing. Character integration is excellent — this could only be Gary.

jokes.text: Gary buying all the pregnancy tests — pharmacy worker: 'I hope you get the result you're looking for.' / Gary: 'No, it's not for me. It's my sister.'
jokes.setup: Gary has bought every pregnancy test in the pharmacy and now faces the pharmacist's knowing comment
jokes.punchline: Gary's cover story — 'it's my sister' — is the most obvious deflection possible, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The 'it's for my sister' deflection is a recognizable comedic device, executed competently. The pharmacist's polite acknowledgment of the obvious lie works. Functional but not elevated.

jokes.text: Gary in the pharmacy: 'I'd hold off on the maternity underwear.'
jokes.setup: Gary is asking if he should also buy maternity underwear for his 'sister' who may or may not be pregnant
jokes.punchline: The pharmacist's flat advice to hold off — delivered as though this is a completely normal consultation
jokes.explanation: Deadpan advice comedy. The pharmacist's calm reasonableness in the face of Gary's chaos is a good straight-man beat, but it's not distinctive enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: Isn't this nice how the lettering works on these... on these tops?
jokes.setup: Gary is desperately trying to fill the awkward silence after his cover story has clearly failed
jokes.punchline: Gary's pivot to admiring the lettering on product packaging — totally non-sequitur — is a perfect portrait of someone imploding under social pressure
jokes.explanation: Great cringe beat. The non-sequitur about 'the lettering on these tops' is specific enough to feel real and the trailing off ('on these... on these tops') shows Gary's brain melting in real time. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'No, it's not impossible. I'm not that old. / All right, you over that bombshell? Because that bombshell just gave birth to a bigger bombshell.'
jokes.setup: Ted says the pregnancy is 'impossible'; Selina defends her age/fertility and then pivots
jokes.punchline: 'That bombshell just gave birth to a bigger bombshell' — the pregnancy metaphor applied to the news itself is a tidy, self-aware construction
jokes.explanation: 'That bombshell just gave birth to a bigger bombshell' is a clever embedded metaphor — pregnancy language applied to the escalating crisis. Economical and shows Selina's quick wit even in crisis.

jokes.text: 'You proposed by giving me some sort of a ring in a heart-shaped, fuckin' oyster thing or something.'
jokes.setup: Selina is spinning a fake engagement story to Ted and needs convincing romantic details
jokes.punchline: Her version of a romantic proposal involves a 'heart-shaped fucking oyster thing' — an aggressively generic and tacky romantic prop that she describes with contempt
jokes.explanation: The 'heart-shaped fucking oyster thing' is a specific and funny object — Selina's disdain for the cliché she herself is inventing is the meta-joke. She can't even commit to a convincing romance narrative.

jokes.text: You proposed by giving me some sort of a ring in a heart-shaped, fuckin' oyster thing or something.
jokes.setup: Selina is manufacturing a cover story for the pregnancy with Ted — they'll claim to have been secretly engaged
jokes.punchline: Selina's placeholder for a romantic proposal is 'a heart-shaped, fuckin' oyster thing' — a vague, contemptuous non-image that reveals how little she's invested in the romance
jokes.explanation: The specific vagueness of 'heart-shaped fuckin' oyster thing' is funny — she needs a romantic cover story but can't be bothered to imagine a plausible one. 'Or something' caps it perfectly. Character-defining.

jokes.text: 'You know, the key is in the details, though, Ted.'
jokes.setup: Selina is constructing a fake engagement story that she's making up on the spot with zero details
jokes.punchline: She emphasizes the importance of details immediately after providing none
jokes.explanation: The irony of demanding detail-consciousness while providing 'heart-shaped oyster thing or something' is light but functional. Good line economy.

jokes.text: 'I don't know how fucking big my finger is, Ted! Just be a man, stand up, get the fuckin' ring, all right?'
jokes.setup: Ted sensibly asks what ring size Selina's finger is so he can buy a ring
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is an escalating tirade — she doesn't know her own ring size and her solution is for Ted to 'just be a man'
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'I don't know how fucking big my finger is' is funny in its absurdly unhelpful specificity — everyone knows their general ring size. Her imperiousness overriding basic problem-solving is character-consistent.

jokes.text: Ken overhears the engagement and immediately offers congratulations: 'I just... you're getting married. I wanted to be the first one to offer my congratulations.'
jokes.setup: Selina has been complaining about Ken's intrusiveness all episode; he's now clearly overheard her fake phone engagement
jokes.punchline: His beaming, sincere congratulations are another violation of Selina's privacy — the first person to know about the fake engagement is the most inappropriately intimate photographer
jokes.explanation: A good payoff for the Ken running gag. His inescapable presence culminates in him being the first witness to the fake engagement — the intruder becomes the confidant. Cringe escalation.

jokes.text: 'Read my lips... Don't read my lips.'
jokes.setup: Selina starts to give Ken a directive using 'read my lips' — then catches herself because Gary can lip-read
jokes.punchline: She reverses the phrase mid-command in a self-aware callback to the lip-reading revelation, resulting in a paradoxical order
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's structural comedic peak. The setup is perfect: 'Read my lips' is a famous political phrase (Bush Sr., 'no new taxes'), deployed naturally in context, then immediately self-corrected. The correction creates a paradox and a callback. Six words of total economy. This is the kind of joke that rewards a second viewing and has a satisfying structural elegance. Genuinely exceptional for a sitcom.

jokes.text: Selina meets Jim Palmer (baseball legend): 'Oh, yes, that is a wonderful thing to be in... in the hall of fame, for sure.'
jokes.setup: Selina is introduced to Hall of Famer Jim Palmer and needs to make impressive baseball small talk
jokes.punchline: Her complete lack of preparation results in the most generic possible observation — that the Hall of Fame is 'a wonderful thing to be in'
jokes.explanation: The social failure of a politician meeting a legend with nothing to say is well-observed. The trailing 'for sure' after her fumbling suggests she knows it's terrible. A nice cringe beat.

jokes.text: 'My guitar is for seduction, not crowd control, all right? Granted, on a good night that's one and the same, but still.'
jokes.setup: Amy suggests Dan use a guitar he spotted to entertain the kids
jokes.punchline: Dan has defined rules about what his guitar is for — seduction — then immediately undercuts the distinction by conceding that crowd-pleasing and seduction sometimes converge
jokes.explanation: Dan's guitar-as-seduction-tool is perfect character work — of course Dan plays guitar only to pick up women. The parenthetical concession ('on a good night that's one and the same') reveals a deeper, funnier truth about Dan's self-image.

jokes.text: Leon in the school: 'She's here yet? No. Of course not, because she hates punctuality almost as much as good governance.'
jokes.setup: Leon West has shown up at the children's event to pursue the story
jokes.punchline: He characterizes Selina's absence as consistent with her hatred of 'punctuality' and 'good governance' — a neat double-barreled political critique delivered casually
jokes.explanation: 'She hates punctuality almost as much as good governance' is a polished, economy-efficient political insult. The 'almost as much as' construction is smartly hierarchical — governance is ranked worse. Sharp journalist-character line.

jokes.text: Dan playing 'Frère Jacques' on guitar for children while crisis unfolds around him
jokes.setup: Dan, the ambitious political operative who uses guitar only for seduction, is forced to entertain children
jokes.punchline: He plays 'Frère Jacques' — a children's song that could not be further from a seduction context — as aides scramble on phones around him
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff of Dan playing Frère Jacques — the exact opposite of seduction music — while looking miserable and surrounded by crisis, is a well-constructed visual callback. The contrast between his self-image and the reality is the joke.

jokes.text: Sue and the fake engagement backstory: 'Children of Courage Awards — Would he have proposed then? / No, no, uh-uh. That's kids with alopecia, their mothers are on crack... that's a complete freak show.'
jokes.setup: Sue is going through Selina's calendar to find a plausible date for the fake engagement
jokes.punchline: The Children of Courage Awards is rejected as a proposal venue because of Selina's blunt characterization of what that event entails
jokes.explanation: Selina's description of the Children of Courage Awards is vicious and funny — 'that's kids with alopecia, their mothers are on crack... that's a complete freak show.' It reveals her true opinion of the charity work she does for optics, and the baldly contemptuous delivery is peak character comedy. The alopecia detail is specific enough to feel real.

jokes.text: Ted proposed to me about six to eight weeks ago, okay? / Oh, congratulations, ma'am. / No, no, no, he didn't really. We're just gonna pretend that he did.
jokes.setup: Selina is briefing Sue on the cover story for the fake engagement
jokes.punchline: Sue's immediate, sincere congratulations — followed by Selina clarifying it's fake — creates a perfect beat where Sue's professional equanimity absorbs a shocking statement without missing a beat
jokes.explanation: Sue's character is well-used here — her robotic professionalism means she congratulates the fake engagement with exactly the same tone she'd use for a real one. The reveal 'we're just gonna pretend that he did' is delivered casually enough to get a laugh.

jokes.text: Dan's guitar lesson for kids: 'You'll figure that out when you get older.' — referring to the mood of minor seventh chords
jokes.setup: Dan is explaining the 'autumn gloom' quality of minor seventh chords to the children
jokes.punchline: 'You'll figure that out when you get older' — implying the children will eventually learn melancholy, which is not a normal thing to tell children
jokes.explanation: Dan teaching children about musical melancholy as a life lesson they'll arrive at through experience is subtle and dark. It's also an accidental portrait of Dan's own jaded interior.

jokes.text: Child's review of Dan's guitar performance: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Dan asks the children if they're having fun after his guitar session
jokes.punchline: One child says simply 'No.' — the flattest, most honest review possible
jokes.explanation: One-word punchline. Maximum economy. Dan's crushing self-regard is punctured by a child who does not have the social skills to soften the blow. 'No.' is funnier than any elaborate put-down would be. Perfect reaction beat.

jokes.text: Dan's response to the child: 'Hear that? No fun. You are no fun.'
jokes.setup: A child has just told Dan his guitar playing is not fun
jokes.punchline: Dan repeats the child's verdict and pivots it into an accusation against the child — 'you are no fun' — rather than accepting the criticism
jokes.explanation: Dan refusing to absorb criticism and redirecting it at an eight-year-old is the most Dan thing possible. His ego protection mechanism working in real time, against a child.

jokes.text: 'Leon West is here. Oh, great. Now all we need are my parents on Skype.'
jokes.setup: Selina arrives at the school event to find Leon West (reporter) is already there
jokes.punchline: She equates Leon's intrusive presence to having her judgmental parents watching via Skype — implying he's as much of a burden as family
jokes.explanation: A nice compression of stress into one image. The Skype parents as ultimate judgment feels true to Selina's anxiety spiral. Economical and character-relevant.

jokes.text: 'We're gonna have more tests than you have pee.'
jokes.setup: Amy is telling Selina that Gary has brought many pregnancy tests
jokes.punchline: She frames it as having more tests than Selina has urine to fill them — a logistical problem framed in the most blunt possible terms
jokes.explanation: 'More tests than you have pee' is a tight, blunt line. The pragmatic calculation — matching test count to urine supply — delivered flatly by Amy is exactly the show's register.

jokes.text: 'Oh, we're gonna have Gary do the tests. / How will that work? / No, not with his pee, Amy.'
jokes.setup: Selina says Gary will 'do the tests'; Amy takes this literally and is briefly confused
jokes.punchline: Selina's clarification — 'not with his pee' — implies Amy's interpretation was plausible enough to need correction
jokes.explanation: The brevity of 'not with his pee, Amy' is the joke — it requires zero explanation beyond the denial. The implication that Amy briefly thought Gary might provide his urine says something about how abnormal Gary's relationship with Selina is.

jokes.text: 'Or a suicide. I haven't decided which.' — Selina on her options regarding the pregnancy
jokes.setup: A staffer refers to the possible wedding that might result from the pregnancy situation
jokes.punchline: Selina offers suicide as an equally valid alternative she's still weighing
jokes.explanation: Selina treating suicide and marriage as equivalent choices with pros and cons is dark and fast. The 'I haven't decided which' is the kicker that sells it — she's genuinely still considering both.

jokes.text: Selina asking for water in a paper cup and getting a large plastic cup: 'This is... okay, it's in a plastic, clear... / It'll be fine. / It'll be fine.'
jokes.setup: Selina needs a paper cup of water to use for a pregnancy test in the bathroom
jokes.punchline: She's given a large plastic cup instead, which creates visible problem for her secret plan — the staff cheerfully reassure her 'it'll be fine' while she stares at the wrong vessel
jokes.explanation: The visual humor of a VP trying to discreetly acquire a small cup for a pregnancy test and getting a comically large one is good cringe setup. The audience knows why she needs it; the staff don't. 'It'll be fine' repeated twice by optimistic strangers who don't understand the problem is a small delight.

jokes.text: Sue: 'How should I know? / You think that as a woman I might sense some disturbance in the fuckin' "lady matrix"?'
jokes.setup: Someone asks Sue (a woman) whether Selina is really pregnant, presumably on the logic that women share some intuitive knowledge
jokes.punchline: Sue's dismissive retort names the assumption — 'the lady matrix' — and demolishes it while being funnier than the assumption itself
jokes.explanation: 'The fuckin' lady matrix' is an outstanding coinage — it names a real and absurd social assumption with perfect precision and fury. Sue's voice is always dry and exasperated, and this line is both a great character beat and a genuinely funny piece of feminist snark. The Matrix sci-fi reference applied to female intuition is original and unexpected. Among the episode's strongest lines.

jokes.text: Ken asking to be Photoshopped into the Orioles photo: 'Ethically, no. I document. I don't invent. Do it yourself.'
jokes.setup: Ken is asked by Gary to Photoshop Gary into the Orioles photo Gary missed
jokes.punchline: Ken — who has been an intruder and irritant all episode — suddenly reveals a fierce professional ethics code: he documents, he doesn't invent
jokes.explanation: The irony is perfect: Ken, who spent the episode violating Selina's privacy and personal space with his camera, turns out to have a strict documentary ethics code about image manipulation. 'I document. I don't invent.' is a great line that recontextualizes Ken as a character.

jokes.text: 'The guy's like gay porn... He's everywhere.' — about Leon West
jokes.setup: The team is surprised Leon West has turned up at the school event
jokes.punchline: Dan compares Leon's ubiquity to gay porn being 'everywhere' on the internet — a crude but specific simile
jokes.explanation: The comparison has a nice logic: Leon is omnipresent the way certain internet content is omnipresent. The specificity of 'gay porn' rather than just 'porn' is a detail that makes the joke sharper. Fast and memorable.

jokes.text: The 'repugnant vs. pregnant' misunderstanding — 'I said she's repugnant. I said Selina's repugnant. / What? Why am I repugnant?'
jokes.setup: The entire team has been managing a pregnancy crisis triggered by Amy mishearing Mike's phone call. Mike said 'repugnant'; Amy heard 'pregnant'
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the entire subplot was based on a phonetic misunderstanding — and Selina immediately pivots to being offended about being called repugnant
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke and the most impressive piece of craft in the episode. A 20-minute setup pays off with a mishearing reveal — 'repugnant' misheard as 'pregnant' — that retroactively reframes the entire episode as a farce built on a phonetic error. The best crisis comedy is crisis-for-nothing, and this is textbook. Then Selina's pivot to taking offense at 'repugnant' is perfect character comedy layered on top. This is genuinely excellent television comedy writing.

jokes.text: Ma'am, if we just table this... I think we should just knock off the kid thing. / Lower that. / We need to get this over with.
jokes.setup: The pregnancy tests are still being conducted even as the 'repugnant' revelation means they might not be necessary; various staffers are trying to manage Selina while she may be taking a test
jokes.punchline: The suggestion to 'knock off the kid thing' — meaning both the children's event and potentially the pregnancy — creates an unintentional double meaning in the chaos
jokes.explanation: More of a transitional beat than a standalone joke. The chaos of multiple storylines colliding is funny but this specific exchange is functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: So pleased to be shaking your hand right at this moment.
jokes.setup: Leon West appears at the school as Selina is emerging from the bathroom where she's been taking pregnancy tests
jokes.punchline: Selina's greeting of Leon — 'so pleased to be shaking your hand right at this moment' — is laden with irony given what she's been doing, what Leon knows, and what Leon doesn't know she knows
jokes.explanation: 'Right at this moment' is the key phrase — it carries maximum contextual irony. Selina performing delight while having just come out of a bathroom pregnancy test is very strong cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Selina apologizing for being late to the children: 'I have never meant those words more.'
jokes.setup: Selina gives the standard 'I'm so sorry I'm late' to the schoolchildren
jokes.punchline: She qualifies it with 'I have never meant those words more' — after the catastrophic morning she's had, this is the most authentic apology she's ever made
jokes.explanation: A nice character moment — Selina's usual performative politeness accidentally becomes genuine. The joke works because the audience knows why she means it more than usual.

jokes.text: But I had to hang out with a bunch of boring grown-ups. / Boo! / Boo! / Boo!
jokes.setup: Selina is apologizing to the children for being late, and mentions she had to be with boring grown-ups
jokes.punchline: The children enthusiastically boo her, turning Selina's attempted crowd-pleasing line into an inadvertent self-indictment
jokes.explanation: The children booing Selina is a nice beat — the crowd she was trying to win over turns against her immediately. Selina performing relatability and having it immediately backfire is quintessential Veep.

jokes.text: Gary on the phone with dad while children sing: 'You're right, I do do a proper man's job.'
jokes.setup: Gary is surrounded by a children's sing-along while trying to convince his father that his job at the VP's office is a real man's job
jokes.punchline: He claims victory — 'I do do a proper man's job' — while audibly surrounded by children singing, which completely undermines the claim
jokes.explanation: The final payoff on Gary's father arc. His triumphant declaration of masculine professional legitimacy while surrounded by children singing 'If You're Happy and You Know It' is both ironic and sad. The structural callback to the father storyline is well-earned by this point in the episode.

jokes.text: Gary's call: 'You're right, I do do a proper man's job.' — closing his father's storyline while 'If You're Happy and You Know It' plays in the background
jokes.setup: Gary's father thinks his job isn't a 'proper man's job'; Gary has spent the episode trying to get an Orioles photo
jokes.punchline: Gary vindicated — but only by a baseball photo, not by anything substantive — while a children's song plays in the background
jokes.explanation: This is the emotional payoff of Gary's entire episode arc. 'I do do a proper man's job' — the stammer-y delivery, the accidental double 'do do,' the desperate validation sought from a dismissive father — while children sing in the background is both funny and genuinely affecting. The children's song as backdrop adds absurdist pathos. Strong structural callback with character integration that only Gary can provide.

jokes.text: 'You're very, very pregnant.' — whispered during children's song
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to maintain a smile and sing with children while her team tries to discreetly handle the pregnancy test situation
jokes.punchline: Someone whispers 'you're very, very pregnant' to Selina mid-children's-song — juxtaposing this devastating news against the cheerful sing-along
jokes.explanation: The timing is everything. 'You're very, very pregnant' delivered during a children's sing-along while Selina forces a smile is a perfectly Veep moment — catastrophic personal and professional news delivered at the least appropriate moment, requiring the maximum of Selina's political composure. The 'very, very' doubling is the exact right level of devastating specificity.

jokes.text: Selina's exit from the school: 'I wish I could be here all day.' — departing immediately after arriving
jokes.setup: Selina has literally just arrived at the school event and spent one minute with the children
jokes.punchline: She performs enthusiastic regret at having to leave — 'I wish I could be here all day' — after what has been the worst day of her VP career
jokes.explanation: Standard political-insincerity comedy but grounded by the context — we know exactly what she's been through and why this is the last place she wants to be. Functional irony.

jokes.text: Staffer's closing observation: 'Waited all day for that.'
jokes.setup: The children and teachers have waited hours for the VP visit; she spent approximately 90 seconds with them
jokes.punchline: 'Waited all day for that.' — four words of perfect deflation on the entire event
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing beat. The episode-long buildup to the VP visit pays off in a single devastatingly flat sentence. Economy: four words. Impact: sums up Selina's entire VP existence. The episode couldn't end better.

tmdb_overview: Selina attends a healthy eating event at Camden Yards with some very grumpy fast-food executives, where she also confides a secret to Amy. Mike holds a press briefing for some local news pals, save for the inquisitive Leon West. Later, with the Veep delayed, Amy and Dan are forced to entertain elementary school children.
insight: Lowest-rated episode relies on cringe discomfort, barely sustaining 69.6 index.
